questions.question: What is the core sequencing of Greenleaf's servant leadership?
options: Lead first; serving the team is a tactic to gain compliance
Serve first; leading is a consequence of serving, not a precondition
Serve and lead are unrelated and chosen independently per situation
Lead first; serve only the highest-performing individuals
questions.explanation: Greenleaf's idea is 'serve first, lead second' — leadership is a consequence of serving, not the other way around. The option framing serving as a tactic for compliance is closer to transactional/command-and-control, which the reviewer explicitly contrasts.

questions.question: What is the crispest distinction the reviewer draws between coaching and mentoring?
options: A coach tells from experience; a mentor asks questions
A coach needs deep domain expertise; a mentor does not
A coach asks so you find your answer; a mentor tells from their experience
A coach owns the agenda; a mentor is content-neutral about the process
questions.explanation: The one-breath version is: coaching = I help you find your answer (no domain expertise required); mentoring = I give you my answer from my experience (same domain). Content-neutrality describes facilitating, not mentoring.

questions.question: In the GROW model, what does the four-letter acronym stand for, in order?
options: Goal, Reality, Options, Will
Goal, Resources, Obstacles, Wrap-up
Growth, Reality, Outcomes, Wisdom
Goal, Reality, Outcomes, Will
questions.explanation: GROW is Goal, Reality, Options, Will. 'Options' (what could you do) is the third stage, not 'Outcomes' or 'Obstacles'.

questions.question: A powerful coaching question is open, short, and forward-moving, and typically starts with **What** or **How** rather than being a closed yes/no question.
questions.explanation: The reviewer defines a powerful question as open, short, and forward-moving, starting with What or How, and warns against closed (yes/no) and leading questions.

questions.question: By the logic, what is the best real-world test of whether your coaching has built a self-sufficient team?
options: How many impediments you personally resolve each sprint
How well the team performs when you are on vacation and not in the room
How closely the team follows every ceremony to the letter
How often the team routes decisions through you for sign-off
questions.explanation: The reviewer says to 'optimize for absence' — the measure is how the team runs when you are away. Being the single point of failure for decisions is the dependency anti-pattern, not success.

questions.question: A production outage is happening at 2am and an engineer needs the fix immediately. Next sprint, in a 1:1, the same person hits the same class of bug. What stance pairing is recommended?
options: Coach during the outage; consult in the 1:1, since urgency has passed
Facilitate during the outage; teach in the 1:1
Consult during the outage; coach in the 1:1, so the learning sticks
Mentor in both cases, since you share your own experience either way
questions.explanation: Push (consult/tell the fix) is fast and right when production is on fire; later, with time, pull (coach) builds the capability so it sticks. Coaching mid-outage is too slow; consulting in the calm 1:1 wastes the chance to build self-sufficiency.

questions.question: Why does the reviewer call facilitating the 'odd one out' among the five stances, and what is its key risk?
options: It requires the deepest domain expertise; the risk is out-coding the team
It is content-neutral; if the facilitator has a stake in the outcome it becomes covert consulting
It is the fastest stance; the risk is building dependence on the facilitator
It transfers a missing fact; the risk is creating passive learners
questions.explanation: Facilitating is content-neutral — the facilitator owns the process, not the outcome. If they have a stake in the result, it degrades into covert consulting. Requiring deep domain expertise and transferring facts describe teaching/consulting, not facilitating.

questions.question: A developer has genuinely never encountered the technique of mocking the clock to fix a timing-dependent test. The stance on whether you should withhold that fact and keep asking questions is:
options: Withhold it — a coach must never give the answer, no matter what
Withhold it, but only escalate to a manager if they stay stuck
Teach the missing fact first, then coach the application; withholding a teachable fact here is cruelty, not coaching
Mentor them by sharing a war story instead, since coaching does not apply
questions.explanation: The reviewer's explicit gotcha: 'not giving the answer' is a default, not an absolute. When someone truly lacks a teachable fact, teach it, then coach the application. Pure-coaching zealotry that withholds needed knowledge is an anti-pattern.

questions.question: The reviewer says GROW is non-linear in practice but that you should always *end* on a particular stage. Which one, and why?
options: End on Reality, because staying there long enough dissolves the problem
End on Options, because the person should leave with many possibilities open
End on Will, so the person leaves with a concrete, owned, by-when commitment
End on Goal, so the desired outcome is the last thing they remember
questions.explanation: You loop back as new Reality details reshape the Goal, but you end on Will with a by-when commitment and a 1-10 confidence check. A conversation that ends without commitment is 'just a nice chat.'

questions.question: At which level of listening does advice-giving 'hide,' ?
options: Level 1 — Internal listening (attention on yourself and what you'll say next)
Level 2 — Focused listening (attention fully on the other person)
Level 3 — Global listening (attention on the whole field and energy)
Advice-giving is impossible at any listening level
questions.explanation: Level 1 is internal — attention on your own reactions and your next line — which is exactly where you listen for 'where can I insert my fix.' Coaches should operate mostly in Levels 2 and 3.

questions.question: What is the mistake of coaching a *Shu*-stage developer with open questions like 'what do you think the right pattern is?'
options: It insults them by treating an expert like a beginner
It is cruel because they lack the foundation to answer; they need teaching, not open questions
It is correct — open questions are always the right move regardless of stage
It violates content-neutrality, turning coaching into covert consulting
questions.explanation: A Shu-stage learner wants concrete rules and recipes; coaching them with open questions just frustrates them — teach them instead. Insulting an expert with a recipe is the opposite error (teaching a Ri-stage person).

questions.question: A Scrum Master must have deep Java expertise to effectively *coach* a Java team.
questions.explanation: Coaching is process expertise, not domain expertise — the team holds the technical answers. Domain knowledge only becomes necessary when the stance shifts to mentoring or teaching.

questions.question: In Lyssa Adkins' agile-coaching competency framing, which statements about 'being vs doing' are correct? Select all that apply.
options: 'Doing' is the techniques and stances; 'being' is your presence, self-awareness, and emotional regulation
Technique (doing) without presence (being) tends to read as manipulation
Presence (being) without technique (doing) is just niceness
'Being' is irrelevant once you have mastered the four stances
Adkins' line is 'your greatest coaching tool is your self'
questions.explanation: Adkins distinguishes doing (techniques/stances) from being (presence, self-awareness, EI). Technique without presence reads as manipulation; presence without technique is mere niceness; you need both — so 'being is irrelevant' is wrong. 'Your greatest tool is your self' is the canonical line.

questions.question: Which of Spears' ten servant-leader characteristics does the reviewer call the 'spine' qualities that keep servant leadership from collapsing into people-pleasing? Select all that apply.
options: Stewardship
Foresight
Healing
Building community
Empathy
questions.explanation: The reviewer names stewardship and foresight as the 'spine' characteristics that prevent servant leadership from becoming people-pleasing. Healing, community, and empathy are the relational/institutional characteristics but are not the named 'spine' pair.

questions.question: An engineer is stuck. Which utterance does the reviewer classify as 'advice-in-disguise' (consulting masquerading as a coaching question) rather than a genuinely powerful question?
options: "What's the real obstacle here?"
"Don't you think you should just refactor it?"
"What have you already tried?"
"What support would move this forward?"
"What were you optimizing for?"
questions.explanation: If a question contains your answer, it is not a coaching question — 'Don't you think you should just refactor it?' is a leading question that smuggles in the answer. The others are open, forward-moving, and answer-free.

questions.question: Read this fragment of a GROW conversation. Which stage are the coach's last two questions exercising, and what is the purpose of the second one?
questions.code:
Coach: "What will you do, and by when?"
Dev:   "Mock the clock in that test by Thursday."
Coach: "On 1-10, how confident are you?"
Dev:   "7."
Coach: "What would make it a 9?"
options: Options stage; the second question generates more alternatives
Reality stage; the second question surfaces the current obstacle
Will stage; the second question is a commitment check that strengthens the action plan toward a higher confidence
Goal stage; the second question redefines what success looks like
questions.explanation: These are Will-stage moves: a concrete by-when action plus a 1-10 commitment check. Asking 'what would make it a 9?' surfaces the support needed (e.g., pairing with Sam) to firm up the commitment — it is not generating new options or redefining the goal.

questions.question: The reviewer describes a 'fading ladder' for building self-sufficiency. What is the correct order of scaffolding steps?
options: Watch them do it -> do it with them -> do it for them -> they own it
Do it for them -> do it with them -> watch them do it -> they own it
They own it -> watch them do it -> do it with them -> do it for them
Do it with them -> do it for them -> they own it -> watch them do it
questions.explanation: The ladder fades the coach out: do-it-for-them, then do-it-with-them, then watch-them-do-it, then they-own-it. Each step removes the coach further; a new challenge can reset the level. The other orderings reverse or scramble the fade.

questions.question: Regarding coaching across the three altitudes (individual, team, organization), which statements are accurate per the reviewer? Select all that apply.
options: Coaching the team means treating it as a system with its own dynamics (trust, conflict norms, shared agreements), not as the sum of its members
Conway's Law is a reason org-level coaching is often the real lever, because architecture is tied to team boundaries
Organizational coaching is mostly influence without authority and change leadership
The same pull-based discipline does not scale up; each altitude needs a fundamentally different discipline
At the team altitude, the unit of change is one person's individual growth
questions.explanation: The team is a system, not a sum of individuals; Conway's Law makes org boundaries the lever for architecture; org coaching is influence-without-authority and change leadership. The same pull discipline DOES scale up (only the system gets larger/slower), and the team altitude's unit of change is the system, not one person.
